elements.sent-2411-001: A substring $u$ of a string $T$ is said to be a repeat if $u$ occurs at least twice in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-002: An occurrence $[i..j]$ of a repeat $u$ in $T$ is said to be a net occurrence if each of the substrings $aub = T[i-1..j+1]$, $au = T[i-1..j+1]$, and $ub = T[i..j+1]$ occurs exactly once in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-003: The occurrence $[i-1..j+1]$ of $aub$ is said to be an extended net occurrence of $u$.
elements.sent-2411-004: Let $T$ be an input string of length $n$ over an alphabet of size $\sigma$, and let $\ENO(T)$ denote the set of extended net occurrences of repeats in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-005: Guo et al. [SPIRE 2024] presented an online algorithm which can report $\ENO(T[1..i])$ in $T[1..i]$ in $O(n\sigma^2)$ time, for each prefix $T[1..i]$ of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-006: Very recently, Inenaga [arXiv 2024] gave a faster online algorithm that can report $\ENO(T[1..i])$ in optimal $O(\#\ENO(T[1..i]))$ time for each prefix $T[1..i]$ of $T$, where $\#S$ denotes the cardinality of a set $S$.
elements.sent-2411-007: Both of the aforementioned data structures can be maintained in $O(n \log \sigma)$ time and occupy $O(n)$ space, where the $O(n)$-space requirement comes from the suffix tree data structure.
elements.sent-2411-008: In particular, Inenaga's recent algorithm is based on Weiner's right-to-left online suffix tree construction.
elements.sent-2411-009: In this paper, we show that one can modify Ukkonen's left-to-right online suffix tree construction algorithm in $O(n)$ space, so that $\ENO(T[1..i])$ can be reported in optimal $O(\#\ENO(T[1..i]))$ time for each prefix $T[1..i]$ of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-010: This is an improvement over Guo et al.'s method that is also based on Ukkonen's algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-011: Further, this leads us to the two following space-efficient alternatives:.
elements.sent-2411-012: A sliding-window algorithm of $O(d)$ working space that can report $\ENO(T[i-d+1..i])$ in optimal $O(\#\ENO(T[i-d+1..i]))$ time for each sliding window $T[i-d+1..i]$ of size $d$ in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-013: A CDAWG-based online algorithm of $O(\e)$ working space that can report $\ENO(T[1..i])$ in optimal $O(\#\ENO(T[1..i]))$ time for each prefix $T[1..i]$ of $T$, where $\e < 2n$ is the number of edges in the CDAWG for $T$.
elements.sent-2411-014: All of our proposed data structures can be maintained in $O(n \log \sigma)$ time for the input online string $T$.
elements.sent-2411-015: We also discuss that the extended net occurrences of repeats in $T$ can be fully characterized in terms of the minimal unique substrings (MUSs) in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-016: Finding repeats in a string is a fundamental task of string processing that has applications in various fields including bioinformatics, data compression, and natural language processing.
elements.sent-2411-017: This paper focuses on the notion of net occurrences of a repeat in a string, which has attracted recent attention.
elements.sent-2411-018: Let $u$ be a repeat in a string $T$ such that $u$ occurs at least twice in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-019: An occurrence $[i..j]$ of a repeat $u$ in $T$ is said to be a net occurrence of $u$ if extending the occurrence to the left or to the right results in a unique occurrence, i.e., each of $aub = T[i-1..j+1]$, $au = T[i-1..j]$, and $ub = T[i..j+1]$ occurs exactly once in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Finding string net occurrences are motivated for Chinese language text processing ~\cite{lin2001extracting,lin2004properties .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The occurrence $[i-1..j+1]$ of $aub$ is said to be an extended net occurrence of a repeat $u$ in $T$, and let $\ENO(T)$ denote the set of all extended net occurrences of repeats in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-022: Guo et al.~\cite{GuoCPM2024 were the first who considered the problem of computing (extended) net occurrences of repeats in a string from view points of string combinatorics and algorithmics.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Guo et al.~\cite{GuoCPM2024 showed a necessary and sufficient condition for a net occurrence of a repeat, which, since then, has played a core role in efficient computation of string net occurrences.}
elements.sent-2411-024: For an input string $T$ of length $n$, they gave an offline algorithm for computing $\ENO(T)$ in $O(n)$ time and space for integer alphabets of size polynomial in $n$, and in $O(n \log \sigma)$ time and $O(n)$ space for general ordered alphabets of size $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-025: Their offline method is based on the suffix array~\cite{ManberM93 and the Burrows-Wheeler transform~\cite{BWT94 .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Ohlebusch et al. gave another offline algorithm that works fast in practice~\cite{OhlebuschBO24 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Later, Guo et al.~\cite{GuoUWZ24 proposed an online algorithm for computing all string net occurrences of repeats.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Their algorithm maintains a data structure of $O(n)$ space that reports $\ENO(T[1..i])$ in $O(n\sigma^2)$ time for each prefix $T[1..i]$ of an online input string $T$ of length $n$.
elements.sent-2411-029: Since their algorithm computes all the net occurrences upon a query, their algorithm requires at least $O(n^2\sigma^2)$ time to maintain and update the list of all (extended) net occurrences of repeats in an online string.
elements.sent-2411-030: Their algorithm is based on Ukkonen's left-to-right online suffix tree construction~\cite{Ukkonen95 , that is enhanced with the suffix-extension data structure of Breslauer and Italiano~\cite{BreslauerI12 .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Very recently, Inenaga~\cite{Inenaga2024-arxiv proposed a faster algorithm that can maintain $\ENO(T[1..i])$ for an online string $T[1..i]$ with growing $i = 1, ..., n$ in a total of $O(n \log \sigma)$ time and $O(n)$ space.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Namely, this algorithm uses only amortized $O(\log \sigma)$ time to update $\ENO(T[1..i])$ to $\ENO(T[1..i+1])$.
elements.sent-2411-033: The proposed algorithm is based on Weiner's right-to-left online suffix tree construction~\cite{Weiner73 that is applied to the reversed input string, and can report all extended net occurrences of repeats in $T[1..i]$ in optimal $O(\#\ENO(T[1..i]))$ for each $1 \leq i \leq n$, where $\#S$ denotes the cardinality of a set $S$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: In this paper, we first show that Ukkonen's left-to-right online suffix tree construction algorithm can also be modified so that it can maintain and update $\ENO(T[1..i])$ in a total of $O(n \log \sigma)$ time with $O(n)$ space, and can report $\ENO(T[1..i])$ in optimal $O(\#\ENO(T[1..i]))$ time for each $i = 1, \ldots, n$.
elements.sent-2411-035: While this complexity of our Ukkonen-based method is the same as the previous Weiner-based method~\cite{Inenaga2024-arxiv , our method enjoys the following merits:.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Our result shows that the arguably complicated suffix-extension data structure of Breslauer and Italiano is not necessary for online computation of string net occurrences with Ukkonen's algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-037: The new method can be extended to the sliding suffix trees~\cite{Larsson96,Senft05,LeonardIBM-arxiv and the compact directed acyclic word graphs (CDAWGs)~\cite{BlumerBHME87,InenagaHSTAMP05 .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The first point is a simplification and improvement over Guo et al.'s method~\cite{GuoUWZ24 based on Ukkonen's construction.}
elements.sent-2411-039: The second point leads us to the following space-efficient alternatives:.
elements.sent-2411-040: A sliding-window algorithm of $O(d)$ working space that can be maintained in $O(n \log \sigma)$ time and can report $\ENO(T[i-d+1..i])$ in optimal $O(\#\ENO(T[i-d+1..i]))$ time for each sliding window $T[i-d+1..i]$ of size $d$ in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-041: A CDAWG-based online algorithm of $O(\e)$ working space that can be maintained in $O(n \log \sigma)$ time and can report $\ENO(T[1..i])$ in optimal $O(\#\ENO(T[1..i]))$ time for each prefix $T[1..i]$ of $T$, where $\e$ is the number of edges in the CDAWG for $T$.
elements.sent-2411-042: We note that $\e < 2n$ always holds~\cite{BlumerBHME87 , and $\e$ can be as small as $O(\log n)$ for some highly repetitive strings~\cite{Rytter06,RadoszewskiR12 .}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Finally, we also discuss that the extended net occurrences of repeats in a string $T$ can be fully characterized with the minimal unique substrings (MUSs)~\cite{IlieS11 in $T$.}

elements.sent-2411-060: A string $w$ is said to be unique in $T$ if $\#\occ_T(w) = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-061: Also, $w$ is said to be quasi-unique in $T$ if $1 \le \#\occ_T(w) \le 2$.
elements.sent-2411-062: Further, $w$ is said to be repeating in $T$ if $\#\occ_T(w) \ge 2$.
elements.sent-2411-063: We denote by $\lrsuf(T)$~(resp.~$\lrpref(T)$) the longest repeating suffix~(resp.~prefix) of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-064: We denote by $\sqsuf(T)$~(resp.~$\sqpref(T)$) the shortest quasi-unique suffix~(resp.~prefix) of $T$.
elements.paragraph-2411-002: Maximal repeats and minimal unique substrings.
elements.sent-2411-065: A repeating substring of a string $T$ is also called a repeat in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-066: A repeat $u$ in $T$ is said to be left-branching in $T$ if there are at least two distinct characters $a,a' \in \Sigma$ such that $\#\occ_T(au) \geq 1$ and $\#\occ_T(a'u) \geq 1$.
elements.sent-2411-067: Symmetrically, a repeat $u$ is said to be right-branching in $T$ if there are at least two distinct characters $b,b' \in \Sigma$ such that $\#\occ_T(ub) \geq 1$ and $\#\occ_T(ub') \geq 1$.
elements.sent-2411-068: A repeat $u$ of $T$ is said to be left-maximal in $T$ if $u$ is a left-branching repeat of $T$ or $u$ is a prefix of $T$, and $u$ is said to be right-maximal in $T$ if $u$ is a right-branching repeat in $T$ or $u$ is a suffix of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-069: A repeat $u$ of $T$ is said to be maximal in $T$ if $u$ is both a left-maximal repeat and a right-maximal repeat in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-070: Let $\LB(T)$, $\RB(T)$, $\LM(T)$, $\RM(T)$, and $\M(T)$ denote the sets of left-branching, right-branching, left-maximal, right-maximal, and maximal repeats in $T$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-072: A unique substring $u = T[i..j]$ of a string $T$ is said to be a minimal unique substring (MUS) of $T$ if each of the substrings $T[i-1..j]$ and $T[i..j-1]$ is a repeat in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-073: By definition, no MUS can be completely contained in another MUS in the string, and thus, there are at most $n$ MUSs in any string $T$ of length $n$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Let $\MUS(T)$ denote the set of occurrences of all MUSs in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-075: In what follows, we fix a string $T$ of length $n > 2$ arbitrarily.

elements.sent-2411-078: Let $\NUS(T)$ be the set of net unique substrings in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-080: Let $\ENO(T)$ be the set of extended net occurrences in $T$.

elements.sent-2411-083: The suffix tree~\cite{Weiner73 of a string $T$ is a compacted trie that represents all suffixes of $T$.}
elements.sent-2411-084: More formally, the suffix tree of $T$ is a rooted tree such that (1) each edge is labeled by a non-empty substring of $T$, (2) the labels of the out-edges of the same node begin with distinct characters, and (3) every suffix of $T$ is represented by a path from the root.
elements.sent-2411-085: If the path from the root to a node $v$ spells out the substring $w$ of $T$, then we say that the node $v$ represents $w$.
elements.sent-2411-086: By representing each edge label $x$ with a pair $(i,j)$ of positions such that $x = T[i..j]$, the suffix tree can be stored in $O(n)$ space.
elements.sent-2411-087: For convenience, we identify each node with the string that the node represents.
elements.sent-2411-088: If $av$ is a node of the suffix tree with $a \in \Sigma$ and $v \in \Sigma^\star$, then the suffix link of the node $av$ points to the node $v$.
elements.sent-2411-089: There are two versions of suffix trees, implicit suffix trees~\cite{Ukkonen95 (a.k.a.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Ukkonen trees) and explicit suffix trees~\cite{Weiner73 (a.k.a Weiner trees).}
elements.sent-2411-091: In the implicit suffix tree of string $T$, each repeating suffix $s$ of $T$ that has a unique right-extension $c \in \Sigma$ (namely, $\#\occ_{T (sc) \geq 1$ and $\#\occ_T(sa) = 0$ for any $a \in \Sigma \setminus \{c\}$) is represented on an edge.}
elements.sent-2411-092: On the other hand, each such repeating suffix is represented by a non-branching internal node in the explicit suffix tree of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-093: Let $\STree'(T)$ and $\STree(T)$ denote the implicit suffix tree and the explicit suffix tree of string $T$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-094: The internal nodes of $\STree'(w)$ represent the right-branching repeats of $T$, while the internal nodes of $\STree(w)$ represent the right-maximal repeats of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-095: It is thus clear that $\STree'(T\$) = \STree(T\$)$ with a unique end-marker $\$$ that does not occur in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-096: Due to the nature of left-to-right online string processing, we will use the implicit suffix trees in our algorithms.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[scale=0.35]{implicit_ST.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.35]{explicit_ST.pdf} \hfil \includegraphics[scale=0.35]{implicit_CDAWG.pdf} \hfil \includegraphics[scale=0.35]{explicit_CDAWG.pdf} \caption{The implicit suffix tree $\STree'(T)$, the explicit suffix tree $\STree(T)$, the implicit CDAWG $\CDAWG'(T)$, and the explicit CDAWG $\CDAWG'(T)$ for string $T = \mathtt{abbbabbabbab}$. The broken arrows represent suffix links. The white and gray stars represent the loci of the longest repeating suffix $\mathtt{bbabbab}$ and shortest quasi-unique suffix $\mathtt{abbab}$ of $T$, respectively.} \label{fig:stree}
elements.sent-2411-097: The compact directed acyclic word graph (CDAWG)~\cite{BlumerBHME87 of a string $T$ is the smallest edge-labeled DAG that represents all substrings of $T$.}
elements.sent-2411-098: There are two versions of CDAWGs as well, implicit CDAWGs~\cite{InenagaHSTAMP05 and explicit CDAWGs~\cite{BlumerBHME87 .}}
elements.sent-2411-099: The implicit CDAWG of string $T$, denoted $\CDAWG'(T)$, is the edge-labeled DAG that is obtained by merging all isomorphic subtrees of the implicit suffix tree $\STree'(T)$ which are connected by suffix links.
elements.sent-2411-100: On the other hand, the explicit CDAWG of $T$, denoted $\CDAWG(T)$, is the edge-labeled DAG that is obtained by merging all isomorphic subtrees of the explicit suffix tree $\STree(T)$ which are connected by suffix links.
elements.sent-2411-101: The internal nodes of $\CDAWG'(T)$ have a one-to-one correspondence with the left-maximal and right-branching repeats in $T$, while the internal nodes of $\CDAWG(T)$ have a one-to-one correspondence with the maximal repeats in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-102: More precisely, the longest string represented by an internal node in $\CDAWG'(T)$ is a left-maximal and right-branching repeat in $T$, and the longest string represented by an internal node in $\CDAWG(T)$ is a maximal repeat in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-103: Thus, as in the case of suffix trees, $\CDAWG'(T\$) = \CDAWG(T\$)$ holds with a unique end-marker $\$$.
elements.sent-2411-104: The length of a path in the implicit/explicit CDAWG is the total length of the labels of the edges in the path.
elements.sent-2411-105: An in-edge of a node $v$ in the implicit/explicit CDAWG is said to be a primary edge if it belongs to the longest path from the source to $v$.
elements.sent-2411-106: Due to the nature of left-to-right online string processing, we will use the implicit CDAWGs in our algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-107: Let $\e'(T)$ and $\e(T)$ denote the number of edges of $\CDAWG'(T)$ and $\CDAWG(T)$, respectively.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Updating extended net occurrences when a character is appended}\label{alg:online} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require ${E} = \ENO(T)$ \Ensure ${E} = \ENO(Tc)$ \Procedure{AppendChar}{String $T$, character $c$, set ${E}$} \If{$\#\occ_{Tc}(\sqsuf(Tc)) = 2$} \label{line:2} \State $(i, j) \leftarrow$ the non-suffix occurrence of $\sqsuf(Tc)$ in $Tc$. \label{line:3} \If{$(i, j+1) \in {E}$} \label{line:4} \State ${E} \leftarrow {E} \setminus \{(i, j+1)\}$ \Comment{by (i) of Lemma~\ref{lem:deletedNOcc}} \label{line:5} \EndIf \EndIf \If{$\#\occ_{Tc}(\lrsuf(Tc)) = 2$} \label{line:8} \State $(i', j') \leftarrow$ the non-suffix occurrence of $\lrsuf(Tc)$ in $Tc$. \label{line:9} \If{$i' > 1$} \If{$(i'-1, j') \in {E}$} \label{line:11} \State ${E} \leftarrow {E} \setminus \{(i'-1, j')\}$ \Comment{by (ii) of Lemma~\ref{lem:deletedNOcc}} \label{line:12} \EndIf \State ${E} \leftarrow {E} \cup \{(i'-1, j'+1)\}$ \Comment{by Lemma~\ref{lem:addedNOccNonsuffix}} \label{line:14} \EndIf \EndIf \If{$|\lrsuf(Tc)| \le |\lrsuf(T)|$} \Comment{$\iff \#\occ_{Tc}(\lrsuf(T)c) = 1$} \label{line:17} \State ${E} \leftarrow {E} \cup \{(|T|-|\lrsuf(T)|, |Tc|)\}$ \Comment{by (i) of Lemma~\ref{lem:addedNOccsuffix}} \label{line:18} \ElsIf{$\#\occ_{T}(c^{\mathit{exp}}) = 1$ where $\mathit{exp} = \max\{e\mid c^e \text{~is a suffix of~} T\}$} \label{line:19} \State ${E} \leftarrow {E} \cup \{(|T|-\mathit{exp}, |Tc|)\}$ \Comment{by (ii) of Lemma~\ref{lem:addedNOccsuffix}} \label{line:20} \EndIf \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-170: From Algorithm~\ref{alg:online , we obtain the following theorem:.}

elements.sent-2411-178: Determine if $(i, j) \in E$ for given pair $(i, j)$.
elements.sent-2411-179: Insert an element $(i, j)$ into $E$ for given pair $(i, j)$.
elements.sent-2411-180: Delete an element $(i, j)$ from $E$ if $(i, j) \in E$ for given pair $(i, j)$.
elements.sent-2411-182: Line~\ref{line:2 uses operation 1, Line~\ref{line:3 uses operation 2, Lines~\ref{line:4 and~\ref{line:11 use operation 7, Lines~\ref{line:5 and~\ref{line:12 use operation 9, Line~\ref{line:8 uses operation 3, Line~\ref{line:9 uses operation 4, Lines~\ref{line:14 , \ref{line:18 , and~\ref{line:20 use operation 8, Line~\ref{line:17 uses operation 5, and Line~\ref{line:19 uses operation 6.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: Thus, Algorithm~\ref{alg:online consists of the above nine operations and basic arithmetic operations.}
elements.sent-2411-184: Note that the correctness of Theorem~\ref{thm:main does not depend on the data structure used.}

elements.sent-2411-186: Based on Ukkonen's left-to-right online suffix tree construction~\cite{Ukkonen95 , we can design a data structure $\mathcal{D _T$ of size $O(|T|)$ that supports all nine operations of Theorem~\ref{thm:main in constant time.}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: The data structure $\mathcal{D _T$ can be updated to $\mathcal{D _{Tc}$ in amortized $O(\log\sigma)$ time where $c$ is a character.}}
elements.sent-2411-188: We employ an implicit suffix tree~\cite{Ukkonen95 of online string $T$ enhanced with the active point, which represents $\lrsuf(T)$, and the secondary active point, which represents $\sqsuf(T)$ as in~\cite{MienoFNIBT22 .}}
elements.sent-2411-189: According to~\cite{MienoFNIBT22 , such an enhanced implicit suffix tree can support operations 1--6 in $O(1)$ time.}
elements.sent-2411-190: For the readers of this paper, we briefly explain how to perform those operations efficiently below:.
elements.sent-2411-191: Operation 5 is obvious since we maintain the active point for every step.
elements.sent-2411-192: Operation 3 can be easily done in constant time by checking whether the active point locates on an edge towards a leaf or not.
elements.sent-2411-193: Operation 1 can be done in constant time by using the (secondary) active points (due to Lemma~1 of~\cite{MienoFNIBT22 ).}
elements.sent-2411-194: Operations 2 and 4 can be done by looking at the leaves under the (secondary) active points.
elements.sent-2411-195: For instance, look at the implicit suffix tree $\STree'(T)$ depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:stree .}
elements.sent-2411-196: The secondary active point (the gray star), which represents the shortest quasi-unique suffix $s = \mathtt{abbab}$, is on an edge towards a leaf.
elements.sent-2411-197: Further, the leaf under the secondary active point represents the suffix $\mathtt{abbabbab}$ of $T$ starting at position $5$.
elements.sent-2411-198: Thus, $s$ occurs at position $5$, which is the non-suffix occurrence of $s$.
elements.sent-2411-199: Similarly, the non-suffix occurrence of the longest repeating suffix $\mathtt{bbabbab}$ is position $3$ since the leaf under the active point (the white star) represents the suffix of $T$ starting at position $3$.
elements.sent-2411-200: Value $\mathit{exp}$ defined in operation 6 can be easily maintained independent of the suffix tree.
elements.sent-2411-201: As for operations 7--9, we implement set $E = \ENO(T)$ as a set of occurrences where each element $(i, j) \in E$ is connected to the corresponding locus of the suffix tree.. Since $T[i..
elements.sent-2411-202: Thus we can perform operations 7--9 in constant time via the leaves of the suffix tree, for given $(i, j)$, which represents some unique substring of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-203: Finally, the data structure can be maintained in amortized $O(\log\sigma)$ time: Basically the amortized analysis is due to~\cite{Ukkonen95 .}
elements.sent-2411-204: The secondary active point, which was originally proposed in~\cite{MienoFNIBT22 , can also be maintained in a similar manner to the active point, and thus the amortized analysis for the secondary active point is almost the same as that for the active point in~\cite{Ukkonen95 ~(see~\cite{MienoFNIBT22 for the complete proof).}}}
elements.sent-2411-205: By wrapping up the above discussions, we obtain the following theorem:.
elements.sent-2411-206: We can compute the set of extended net occurrences of string $T$ of length $n$ given in an online manner in a total of $O(n \log\sigma)$ time using $O(n)$ space.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Sliding-window algorithm based on implicit suffix trees

elements.sent-2411-218: On the other hand, our scheme is also applicable to a sliding-window of variable size.
elements.sent-2411-220: We can maintain the set of extended net occurrences for a sliding window over string $T$ of length $n$ in a total of $O(n \log\sigma)$ time using $O(d)$ working space where $d$ is the maximum size of the window.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Online algorithm based on implicit CDAWGs
elements.sent-2411-221: The next lemma is an adaptation of Lemma~\ref{lem:Ukkonen which uses implicit CDAWGs in place of implicit suffix trees:.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Based on the left-to-right online CDAWG construction~\cite{InenagaHSTAMP05 , we can design a data structure $\mathcal{C _T$ of size $O(\e(T))$ that supports all nine operations of Theorem~\ref{thm:main in constant time.}}}
elements.sent-2411-223: The data structure $\mathcal{C _T$ can be updated to $\mathcal{C _{Tc}$ in amortized $O(\log\sigma)$ time where $c$ is a character.}}
elements.sent-2411-224: Since the online implicit CDAWG construction algorithm~\cite{InenagaHSTAMP05 is based on Ukkonen's implicit suffix tree construction, it also maintains the active point that indicates the locus corresponding to $\lrsuf(T)$.}
elements.sent-2411-225: While the locus can correspond to multiple substrings of $T$ (as the CDAWG is a DAG), we can retrieve $|\lrsuf(T)|$ in $O(1)$ time by storing, in each node $v$ of $\CDAWG'(T)$, the length of the maximal repeat corresponding to $v$.
elements.sent-2411-226: This is because the path that spells out $\lrsuf(T)$ from the source consists only of the primary edges (see~\cite{InenagaHSTAMP05 for more details).}
elements.sent-2411-227: Since edge label $x$ is represented by an integer pair $(p,q)$ such that $x = T[p..q]$, we can obtain the non-suffix occurrence $(i',j')$ of $\lrsuf(T)$ in $O(1)$ time (Line~\ref{line:9 in Algorithm~\ref{alg:online ).}}
elements.sent-2411-228: The secondary active point that indicates the locus for $\sqsuf(T)$ can also be maintained on the implicit $\CDAWG'(T)$ by adapting the algorithm from~\cite{MienoFNIBT22 .}
elements.sent-2411-229: Let $y$ be the suffix of $T$ that is one-character shorter than $\sqsuf(T)$.
elements.sent-2411-230: By definition, $y$ is the longest suffix of $T$ such that $\#\occ_T(y) \geq 3$.
elements.sent-2411-231: Given the locus $P$ for $y$ on $\CDAWG'(T)$, one can check in $O(1)$ time whether the substrings corresponding to $P$ occur at least 3 times, by checking the number of paths from $P$ to the sink and checking if the active point is in the subgraph under $P$.
elements.sent-2411-232: Also, by definition, $y$ is the longest string represented by the locus $P$.
elements.sent-2411-233: This tells us the length of $\sqsuf(T)$ as well.
elements.sent-2411-234: Thus, we can also maintain the secondary active point in a similar manner to the active point on the implicit CDAWG in $O(\log \sigma)$ amortized time per character, and we can obtain the non-suffix occurrence $(i,j)$ of $\sqsuf(T)$ in $O(1)$ time (Line~\ref{line:3 in Algorithm~\ref{alg:online ).}}
elements.sent-2411-236: We can compute the set of extended net occurrences of string $T$ of length $n$ given in an online manner in a total of $O(n \log\sigma)$ time using $O(\e(T))$ working space.

elements.sent-2411-239: Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:implicit_CDAWG_size , we have $\e'(T[1..i]) \leq \e'(T) \leq \e(T)$ for any $1 \leq i \leq n$, which leads to an $O(\e(T))$-space bound.}
elements.section-2411-005: Relating extended net occurrences and MUSs
elements.sent-2411-240: In this section, we give a full characterization of the extended net occurrences of repeats in $T$ in terms of minimal unique substrings (MUSs) in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-242: Let $[i-1..h], [k..j+1] \in \MUS(T)$ be the intervals that represent consecutive MUSs in $T$, namely, there is no element $[s..t]$ in $\MUS(T)$ such that $i \leq s < k$ and $h < t \leq j$.
elements.sent-2411-243: Then, $[i..j]$ is a net occurrence for repeat $u = T[i..j]$.
elements.sent-2411-244: Observe that any unique substring of $T$ must contain a MUS in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-245: Since $T[i..j]$ does not contain a MUS, $u = T[i..j]$ is a repeat in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-247: Then, $\#\occ_T(aub) = \#\occ_T(au) = \#\occ_T(ub) = 1$ since any of $aub = T[i-1..j-1]$, $au = T[i-1..j]$, and $ub = T[i..j+1]$ contains a MUS.
elements.sent-2411-248: A consequence of Lemma~\ref{lem:ENO_MUS is that a net occurrence $[i..j]$ cannot be contained in another net occurrence $[i'..j']$.}
elements.sent-2411-249: This is because a MUS cannot be contained in another MUS.
elements.sent-2411-250: Another consequence is that two consecutive extended net occurrences in $\ENO(T)$ are overlapping.
elements.sent-2411-251: Below we show the reversed version of Lemma~\ref{lem:ENO_MUS :.}

elements.sent-2411-256: Similarly, there is a MUS $[i..y]$ with $y \le k-1$ that starts at $i$.

elements.sent-2411-258: If $i < x$, there are at least two MUSs within range $[i, j] \subset [h, k]$.
elements.sent-2411-259: If $i > x$, there are at least two MUSs within range $[x, y] \subset [h, k]$.
elements.sent-2411-260: In both cases, there exists some net occurrence within range $[h, k]$ by Lemma~\ref{lem:ENO_MUS , which contradicts that $[h..j]$ and $[i..k]$ are consecutive elements in $\mathsf{L}$.}

elements.sent-2411-273: We can maintain the set of all MUSs of a string $T$ of length $n$ given in an online manner in a total of $O(n \log\sigma)$ time using $O(\e(T))$ working space, where $\e(T)$ denotes the size of $\CDAWG(T)$.

elements.sent-2411-275: This can easily be maintained in the implicit CDAWG as follows.
elements.sent-2411-276: Let $z$ be the node of $\CDAWG'(T)$ from which the primary edge to the sink stems out.
elements.sent-2411-277: We identify $z$ with the maximal repeat that the node represents.
elements.sent-2411-278: If the active point does not exist on this primary edge, then $|z| = |\lrpref(T)|$.
elements.sent-2411-279: If the active point lies on this primary edge leading to the sink, then $|z|+k = |\lrpref(T)|$, where $k$ is the offset of the active point on the primary edge from the node $z$.
elements.sent-2411-283: In this paper we presented how Ukkonen's left-to-right online suffix tree construction can be used for online computation of string net frequency.
elements.sent-2411-284: Our main contributions are space-efficient algorithms for computing string net occurrences, one works in $O(d)$ space in the sliding model for window-length $d$, and the other works in $O(\e(T))$ space where $\e(T)$ denotes the size of the CDAWG of the input string $T$.
elements.sent-2411-285: Both of our methods run in $O(n \log \sigma)$ time and can report all (extended) net occurrences of repeats in the current string in output-optimal time.
elements.sent-2411-286: We also showed that computing the sorted list of extended net occurrences of repeats in a string $T$ is equivalent to computing the sorted list of minimal unique substrings (MUSs) in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-287: An intriguing open question is whether one can efficiently compute the extended net occurrences of repeats within $O(\rb)$ space, where $\rb$ denotes the number of equal-character runs in the BWT of the input string.
elements.sent-2411-288: It is known that $\rb \leq \e$ holds for any string~\cite{BelazzouguiCGPR15 .}
elements.sent-2411-289: The R-enum algorithm of Nishimoto and Tabei~\cite{NishimotoT21 is able to compute the set of MUSs in $O(n \log \log_\omega (n/\rb))$ time with $O(\rb)$ space, where $\omega$ denotes the machine word size of the word RAM model.}
elements.sent-2411-290: However, it is unclear whether their algorithm can output a list of MUSs arranged in the sorted order of the beginning positions within $O(\rb)$ space.
